sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Adrenal insufficiency can present as adrenal crisis with hypotension, shock, hypoglycaemia, electrolyte disturbance, and altered conscious state. Prehospital priorities are early recognition, treatment of reversible threats such as hypoglycaemia, protocol-led steroid replacement where available, and urgent transport.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Known adrenal insufficiency or chronic steroid dependence with acute deterioration
- Weakness, lethargy, dizziness, abdominal pain, vomiting, or diarrhoea
- Hypotension, postural collapse, shock, or syncope
- Hypoglycaemia, confusion, reduced conscious state, or seizure
- Fever or intercurrent illness as a common precipitant

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Sepsis, dehydration, hypoglycaemia, and hyperkalaemia may coexist
- A patient or carer may have emergency steroid medication or an established action plan
- Do not delay transport while searching for a perfect diagnosis if shock or altered conscious state is present
- Consider adrenal crisis in unexplained collapse or refractory hypotension, especially in patients with known endocrine disease or chronic steroid exposure

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Check BGL early and treat hypoglycaemia promptly within scope
- Give oxygen only if clinically indicated
- Keep the patient warm and perform frequent reassessment of blood pressure, conscious state, respiratory status, and perfusion
- Obtain medication history, trigger history, and any patient-held steroid emergency plan if this does not delay care
- Prioritise urgent transport for shock, reduced conscious state, recurrent vomiting, or severe weakness

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- ECG
- Gain IV access early if adrenal crisis is suspected
- Administer hydrocortisone only within local protocol, scope, or senior direction
- Provide IV crystalloid therapy where hypotension or shock is present, with repeated reassessment
- Treat hypoglycaemia with oral, IV, or IM therapy according to the patient's condition and local protocol
- Escalate early if shock, hyperkalaemia, refractory hypoglycaemia, or altered conscious state is present

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Known adrenal-insufficient patients may deteriorate rapidly during illness, trauma, or prolonged vomiting
- Record baseline steroid therapy if known, BGL trend, blood pressure response, and whether emergency hydrocortisone was available or given
- When hyperkalaemia is suspected, integrate management with the hyperkalaemia guideline rather than treating this as an isolated endocrine problem
